coredata.dc:title: Impact of thickness and charge rate on the electrochemical performance of Si-based electrodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Shuanglong
Zhou, Junwu
Tan, Bowen
Zheng, Bailin
Zhang, Kai
coredata.dc:description: Thick electrodes with high-capacity materials are a key strategy for increasing lithium-ion battery energy density, but they face challenges like mechanical instability and sluggish electron-ion transport kinetics. In silicon electrodes, volume expansion alters the porous structure, affecting lithium-ion pathways and generating internal stresses. This study introduces a 2D particle packing model incorporating electrochemical-mechanical coupling and lithiation-induced porosity changes to analyze the performance of Si electrodes with varying thicknesses at different C-rates. Results indicate that internal stresses impact diffusion and reaction rates. Thick electrodes show increased concentration polarization, uneven stress, interfacial delamination, and damage to the carbon-binder domain at high C-rates. Capacity retention rates vary little across C-rates for the same thickness but differ significantly with varying electrode thicknesses as C-rates increase, consistent with experimental results. The model enhances the understanding of thick Si electrode cycling performance, aiding in designing higher-energy-density Si-based electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coupled electro-chemo-mechanical model
particle packing model
thick electrode
C-rate performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Multi-field coupling model of Si-based half-cell
(A) Multi-field coupling relationships of particle scale.
(B) Multi-field coupling relationships of electrode scale.
The structure of coin cells and the internal ion and electron transport mechanisms
(A) The internal structure of a coin half-cell includes components such as a spring gasket, metal gasket, separator, silicon electrode, and Cu foil.
(B) The migration process of Li-ions and Li-electrons in a Si electrode half-cell during discharge, as well as the flow direction of electrons and current in the external circuit.
The changes in active particle parameters affected by stress
(A) The stress distribution in Si particles (A-1); the Li concentration distribution (A-2) without considering the stress effect; and (A-3) considering the stress effect.
(B) The variation of total reaction current of particle surface under stress influence at C-rate = 0.5 C.
(C) The variation of effective diffusion coefficient under stress influence at C-rate = 0.5 C.
The concentration variations within the active electrode

(A) Li-ion concentration distribution status in active particles of different thicknesses electrodes (10, 20, and 40 μm) at the separator side, mid-section of the active electrode, and current collector side.
(B) Li insertion state disparity between active particles near the separator side and those near the current collector side, with different thicknesses of electrodes (10, 20, and 40 μm).
(C) Spatial and contour distributions of electrolyte salt on electrodes with different thicknesses (10, 20, and 40 μm) under different C-rates (0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C).
EIS results of the electrode
(A) Nyquist plot of the electrode in the charged state after five formation cycles and after 50 charge-discharge cycles.
(B) The specific surface area statistics of Si particles in the PPM.
The stress state of the electrode
(G) von Mises stress distribution on the edge side of the electrode at different C-rates (0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C) for a 40-μm-thick electrode, and at different thicknesses (10, 20, and 40 μm) at 0.5 C.
The stress distribution on the CBD
(A) von Mises stress of CBD at different C-rates (0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C) of 10-μm electrode and different thicknesses (10, 20, and 40 μm) at 0.5 C.
(B) Probability of damage in the CBD at different C-rate (0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C) of 10-μm electrode and different thicknesses (10, 20, and 40 μm) at 0.5 C.
Optical microscope images of Si surface morphology
(A) Surface morphology of the unlithiated Si electrode sheet and its localized magnified morphology.
(B and C) Surface morphology of (B) 10 μm and (C) 20 μm Si electrodes after 50 cycles at 0.5 C. The scale bars in (A)–(C) are 200 μm.
(D) Overall morphology of 40 μm Si electrode after 50 cycles at 0.5 C.

(E) Distribution of by-products of electrolyte decomposition on Li-metal foil corresponding to different thicknesses (10, 20, and 40 μm) of active electrodes, obtained after disassembling the CR2032-type coin cell after 50 cycles at 0.5 C.
Capacity degradation curve of the Si-based coin half-cell
(A–C) The capacity retentions of Si-based coin cells after 200 cycles were investigated at C-rates (0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C) under the same thickness (A, 10 μm; B, 20 μm; C, 40 μm).
(D–F) The capacity retentions of Si-based coin cells after 200 cycles were investigated at different thicknesses (10, 20, and 40 μm) under the same C-rate (D, 0.1 C; E, 0.3 C; F, 0.5 C).

head.ce:title: Impact of thickness and charge rate on the electrochemical performance of Si-based electrodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Aerospace Engineering and Applied Mechanics, Tongji University, No. 1239 Siping Road, Shanghai 200092, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Aerospace Engineering and Applied Mechanics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Aerospace Engineering and Applied Mechanics, Tongji University, No.1239 Siping Road, Shanghai 200092, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thick electrodes with high-capacity materials are a key strategy for increasing lithium-ion battery energy density, but they face challenges like mechanical instability and sluggish electron-ion transport kinetics. In silicon electrodes, volume expansion alters the porous structure, affecting lithium-ion pathways and generating internal stresses. This study introduces a 2D particle packing model incorporating electrochemical-mechanical coupling and lithiation-induced porosity changes to analyze the performance of Si electrodes with varying thicknesses at different C-rates. Results indicate that internal stresses impact diffusion and reaction rates. Thick electrodes show increased concentration polarization, uneven stress, interfacial delamination, and damage to the carbon-binder domain at high C-rates. Capacity retention rates vary little across C-rates for the same thickness but differ significantly with varying electrode thicknesses as C-rates increase, consistent with experimental results. The model enhances the understanding of thick Si electrode cycling performance, aiding in designing higher-energy-density Si-based electrodes.

Geng et al. introduce a multiscale, multiphysics coupled, two-dimensional particle packing model that accounts for porosity changes induced by lithiation. This model is used to analyze the performance of Si electrodes with varying thicknesses under different C-rates, aiding in the design of Si-based electrodes with high energy density.

ce:section.ce:para:
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) serve as significant energy storage tools in modern society, widely employed in consumer electronics and electric vehicles due to their high energy density, compact size, and long-cycle life. 1
,
2
,
3 With the increasing demand for higher energy-density LIBs, researchers aim to enhance battery energy density by increasing the thickness of the active electrode, which serves as the primary Li-ion storage component. 4 From the perspective of overall cell structure, thick electrode design methods significantly increase the loading of active materials and reduce the proportion of inactive components (e.g., electrolytes, separators), thus enhancing battery energy density while lowering costs. A study 5 has shown that increasing the electrode thickness from 25 (with an active material loading of 8 mg/cm2) to 200 μm (with an active material loading of 64 mg/cm2) reduces the proportion of inactive materials from 44% to 12%, greatly improving the proportion of active electrode materials and effectively enhancing the overall energy density of the battery. Additionally, Si as an electrode material has a theoretical capacity of 4,200 mAh/g, making it an ideal candidate for future high-capacity electrodes. Combined with the design of thick electrodes, it can significantly enhance the energy density of batteries, fulfilling the long-standing desire for high-capacity electrodes.

The utilization of thicker electrodes presents a dual-edge scenario: it yields augmented capacity yet simultaneously engenders issues related to capacity decay, demanding meticulous scrutiny and consideration. With the increase in battery thickness, numerous challenges arise, including severe mechanical instability and sluggish electron-ion transport kinetics often faced by thick electrodes. These issues result in significant capacity loss during battery operation.

In the study of electron-ion transport kinetics, Danner et al. 6 employed a detailed three-dimensional microstructure analysis model to investigate the limiting factors of thick NMC (Ni-Mn-Co)-graphite LIB performance and found strong restrictions on Li-ion transport in the electrolyte at high rates, leading to significant capacity losses. However, the uneven distribution of conductive carbon black (CB) does not affect electrode performance. Park et al. 7 studied LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2 electrodes and found that charge transport limitations are primarily due to Li-ion diffusion rather than electronic conduction in thick electrodes. As cycling progresses, negative feedback between accumulated ion transport restrictions and increased local resistance leads to severe reaction non-uniformity in thick electrodes. This results in the generation of current hotspots within the electrode and corresponding localized material degradation, further inhibiting charge transport and causing inevitable capacity decay. To address the issue of sluggish electron-ion transport kinetics, researchers have designed porous electrodes with vertical channels 8
,
9
,
10 and implemented active material gradients 4
,
11 to improve ion transport pathways in thick electrodes. The mechanical instability is primarily manifested through cracking, pulverization, and delamination of the active electrode. These issues result in the formation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), loss of electrode active material, and increased interfacial resistance. 12
,
13
,
14
,
15
,
16
,
17 For Si electrodes, the inherent 300% volume expansion rate exacerbates these problems. It causes mutual compression among active electrodes, leading to the collapse of internal voids in the porous structure, which hinders Li-ion transport pathways. Moreover, this expansion generates greater stress on the electrode, making Si-thick electrodes significantly more mechanically unstable. Research indicates that to avoid significant mechanical stress within Si electrodes and ensure effective channels for Li-ion transport, the porosity of active electrodes is much higher compared with conventional graphite electrodes. 18 In addition, Si-active electrodes exhibit significant changes in modulus during lithiation, thereby influencing the mechanical properties of the active electrodes. 19
,
20 Therefore, it is necessary to conduct studies on the mechanical stability and electron-ion transport kinetics of thick Si electrodes.

The mechanical stability and electron-ion transport kinetics within the electrode are not independent factors; rather, a complex coupling relationship exists between them. Under constant temperature conditions, the internal environment of active electrodes is a complex multi-physical field coupling involving mechanics, chemistry, and electricity. Variations in electrode thickness result in significant differences in stress and concentration distributions on the active electrode, leading to discrepancies in the electrochemical and mechanical performance of the electrode. The multi-field coupling issue in active electrodes has been studied extensively by numerous scholars. 21
,
22
,
23
,
24 Zhao et al. 21 summarized the electro-chemo-mechanical issues at the scale of active particles, electrodes, and batteries to better understand changes in battery performance and capacity loss. Song et al. 22 proposed a fully coupled electrochemical-mechanical theoretical model that incorporates species diffusion, interface transfer kinetics, and mechanical deformation, studying the impact of bending deformation on electrode lithiation capability and capacity. Hofmann et al. 23 employed a phase-field approach to address the coupling of electrochemical diffusion models with small strain elastic-plastic models of Li-ion phosphate particles. This coupling considers the mechanical expansion of the lattice during intercalation processes. The electrochemical-mechanical coupling is derived from the Helmholtz free energy, resulting in constitutive equations for diffusion and mechanical equilibrium within electrode materials. Based on the complex coupling relationship within the electrode, it is necessary to construct a clear model to explain the electron-ion transport dynamics and mechanical stability issues in thick Si electrodes.

The particle packing model (PPM) enables manual construction of the microstructure of active electrodes based on particle shape and distribution patterns. Compared to the pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model, the PPM provides detailed features of electrode microstructure, including spatial particle positions, interface geometrical parameters, and more. 25
,
26 These features assist researchers in analyzing electrode electrochemical and mechanical performance from mesoscopic and microscopic perspectives. Additionally, the PPM model can account for the interactions between ions, such as the uniform stress distribution caused by mutual compression of particles. This stress non-uniformity alters the electrochemical reactions 27 on the particle surfaces and diffusion rates in solid-phase particles. 28 There is also the alteration of the porous structure caused by the mutual compression between particles. These features are not represented in the P2D model. Thus, the PPM model has unique advantages in analyzing the mechanical and electrochemical performance of thick Si electrodes.

This study conducted a cycling performance test experiment on Si electrodes at varying thicknesses and rates. The mechanisms behind the cycling performance changes of Si electrodes at different thicknesses and rates were analyzed by developing a coupled field PPM model and an equivalent electrode model that incorporates mechanical, chemical, and electrical aspects. The research methodology and conclusions guide designing high-capacity, high-performance Si electrode batteries.

The lithiation process of Si within the battery exhibits a complex multi-scale and multi-field coupling relationship, as illustrated in Figure 1 . At the particle scale (as shown in Figure 1A), the lithiation process in Si particles leads to material softening and significant volumetric expansion. This expansion is constrained by the surrounding carbon-binder domain (CBD) and Si, resulting in a stress field on the Si particles that varies with concentration and modulus. This stress includes diffusion-induced stress and stress caused by squeezes. The stress field on the Si particle surface affects the surface reaction current, while the internal stress field influences the Li-ion diffusion coefficient in the solid phase, both of which in turn impact the concentration distribution in the solid phase. At the electrode scale (as shown in Figure 1B), the volumetric expansion of Si particles alters the porosity of the active electrode, affecting the diffusion rate of Li-ions in the electrolyte, the electrolyte conductivity, and the solid-phase conductivity, thus influencing the electrolyte salt concentration distribution within the active electrode. The concentration distribution of the electrolyte salt impacts the cell potential, and the equilibrium potential of the solid phase, which varies with the Li concentration in the Si particles, also affects the cell potential. Changes in cell potential influence the overpotential on the surface of the active particles, thereby affecting the reaction current on the Si particle surfaces and ultimately the concentration distribution on the Si particles. Additionally, the volumetric expansion of Si particles induces substantial volume deformation in the active electrode, leading to mechanical instability at the electrode scale, which, in turn, affects the lithiation state of the electrode. From this analysis, it is evident that the coupled fields within the battery are established around the Li-ion concentration in the Si particles, with the expansion due to lithiation being a crucial factor linking concentration, stress, and porosity. Based on the aforementioned coupling relationships, this study establishes a multi-scale mechano-electrochemical coupling equation system. The derivation of these coupling equations is provided in Notes S1 and S2.

A Si-based half-cell consists of a Si active electrode as the cathode and a Li-metal anode, with a separator between the two electrodes. The electrolyte fills the space inside the coin cell casing. The schematic structure of a Si-based coin half-cell is shown in Figure 2 A. LIBs generate electric current in external circuits by the movement of Li-ions between the cathode and anode active materials. During the discharge process (as shown in Figure 2B) of a Si-based half-cell, metallic Li in the negative electrode undergoes oxidation, losing electrons to form Li-ions. These ions then migrate through the separator to the positive electrode, where they undergo reduction reactions on the surface of Si particles, forming intercalation compounds with Si. During charging, Li dissociates from Si particles to form Li-ions, which then return to the negative electrode through the separator, where they are reduced back to metallic Li on the Li-metal surface. The migration of Li-ions in the electrolyte within the interstitial space of the active electrode is mainly influenced by concentration gradients and potential under isothermal conditions. Li-ions traverse a convoluted path through the active electrode to reach the surface of Si particles, and the migration resistance of Li-ions leads to increased concentration gradients on both sides of the active electrode. Changes in ion concentration occur near the surface of Si particles due to electrochemical reactions during charge and discharge processes. Electron migration primarily occurs through the CBD, with electrons distributing more rapidly and uniformly in the thickness direction of the electrode compared with Li-ion migration speed. The diffusion speed of ions in the electrolyte lags behind the changing Li-ion concentration near the surface of Si particles, resulting in concentration gradients in the electrolyte near the surface of Si particles. Concentration gradients on the electrode contribute to internal polarization resistance in the battery, leading to the generation of wasted heat and a reduction in voltage between the positive and negative electrodes after connection to the circuit.

The Li-ions migrating to the surface of the active particles undergo electrochemical reactions and become embedded within them. Considering only diffusion, the distribution of Li within the particles forms a concentric circular gradient, as shown in Figure 3A-2. However, this does not reflect the actual situation. In fact, due to the compression between particles, the distribution of Li-ions within the particles becomes uneven, as shown in Figure 3A-3. From Figures 3A-1 and 3A-3, it can be observed that the concentration distribution is correlated with the stress distribution. This is because the compression alters the amount of Li-ions embedded in the particles, which in turn affects the electrochemical properties of the active electrode. Fang et al. 29 also found in their research that diffusion stress is a priority due to its significant impact on ionic diffusion within particles. The traditional P2D model does not account for the stress-induced changes in the concentration distribution of the particles. Hence, the PPM model, which considers stress coupling, more accurately depicts the Li-ion distribution state and electrochemical properties of electrode particles.

Figure 3B and 3C reveals that stress exerts a significant influence on the effective diffusion coefficient and electrochemical reaction rate on active particle surfaces, which is also a primary factor contributing to the uneven distribution of Li-ions within these active particles. However, it is noteworthy that in regions subjected to higher pressure, the effective diffusion coefficient and electrochemical reaction rates of particles decrease. This is because stress alters the free energy of the system, which in turn changes the diffusion capability of the active particles and the electrochemical reaction performance on the surface of the active particles. As a result, both the total reaction current at the particle surface and the solid-phase diffusion coefficient vary with the change in stress. From the perspective of relative change, the ratio of surface current in areas under higher stress compared to areas under lower stress is larger, whereas this ratio is smaller for the diffusion coefficient. This is consistent with Lu and coworkers’ research, 30 which revealed that compressive hydrostatic stress reduces chemical activity, thereby hindering the intercalation of Li-ions on the surface of Si particles.

Figure 4 A shows the Li-ion concentration distribution in active particles located near the current collector side, near the separator side, and at the center position of electrodes at t = 3,600 s under a 0.5 C-rate with different thicknesses. It demonstrates that the particle concentration distributions at the three locations are similar; however, the concentration values of the particles differ. This is because the particle distributions at the three positions are identical. During the expansion process, the compressive effects exerted by the particles on their surrounding environment are similar, resulting in comparable concentration distributions. Nevertheless, differences in the electrochemical reaction rates on the particle surface at the three locations lead to variations in the concentration values. As the electrode thickness increases from 10 to 20 to 40 μm, the particles in the 40-μm electrode exhibit higher Li-ion concentrations. However, the concentration difference between the separator side and the current collector side also becomes significant. Although the Li-ion concentration in particles of the 10-μm electrode is lower compared with that in the 40-μm electrode, the concentration difference between the separator side and the current collector side is smaller. This indicates that thicker electrodes have a larger Li-ion concentration gradient of solid-phase material in the thickness direction.

To better analyze the variations in Li-ion concentrations of particles across electrodes of different thicknesses in the thickness direction, the disparity between the Li insertion states in the active electrode on the separator side (c 1) and the current collector side (c 2) can be expressed as
Δ c =
c 1
−
c 2
. Based on the aforementioned difference in Li insertion states, the variation over time of the Li-ions insertion states on the separator side and the current collector side of the active electrode, under different thicknesses, is illustrated in Figure 4B. The figure illustrates that the Li-ion insertion states on active particles near the separator side and those near the current collector side exhibit a significant difference during the middle-to-later stages of Li-ions insertion. In contrast, this difference is smaller at the initial and final stages, and it becomes more pronounced on thicker electrodes. This phenomenon arises from the disparity in electron and Li-ion diffusion rates during the Li-ion insertion process. Electrons, with their faster transfer speed, swiftly distribute throughout the entire active electrode, while Li-ions, with their slower diffusion rate compared with electrons, cannot uniformly distribute rapidly across the electrode thickness. As Li-ions in the electrolyte are consumed, the incoming replenished Li-ions are primarily consumed by active particles near the separator side, leading to Li-ion aggregation at the separator side. Active particles near the current collector side receive fewer migrating Li-ions due to the longer migration distance, resulting in lesser Li-ion insertion on these particles. Over time, this difference becomes more pronounced. When the Li-ion concentration on active particles near the separator side approaches saturation, more Li-ions can migrate to active particles near the current collector side, thereby reducing the concentration disparity between the two sides of the active electrode. Due to the different thicknesses of the active electrodes, the degree of Li-ion insertion varies between the two ends. A higher proportion of electrochemical reactions occurs at the top positions near the separator side, while reactions are limited near the bottom positions near the current collector. As the cycling progresses, thicker electrodes exhibit increasingly deteriorated performance, with a growing current density at the top leading to the formation of hotspots, consequently damaging the active materials and causing cracks or consumption of electrolytes.

In the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of coin cells with 10-, 20-, and 40-μm-thick electrodes, contrary to intuitive expectations, the thicker electrodes exhibited smaller charge transfer resistance. The EIS results for the 10-, 20-, and 40-μm electrodes are shown in Figure 5 . Here, T1, T2, and T3 represent electrodes with thicknesses of 10, 20, and 40 μm, respectively. It can be observed that the charge transfer resistance decreases in the order of T1>T2>T3. However, the ohmic resistance (high-frequency region of the EIS plot) shows the opposite trend, with T3>T2>T1. The explanation for this result is that thicker electrodes have a larger specific surface area (SSA), providing Si particles with a greater reactive SSA. This leads to higher charge transfer capabilities in thicker electrodes. While thick electrodes exhibit smaller charge transfer resistance, their performance is significantly limited by increased ohmic resistance and ionic migration resistance. The Nyquist plots after 50 charge-discharge cycles reveal that the charge transfer resistance of electrodes of all thicknesses has increased. However, the order of resistance magnitude remains T1>T2>T3. The increase in charge transfer resistance is attributed to the continuous formation of the SEI layer, which increases the difficulty of charge transfer at the interface. 31
,
32 The ohmic resistance still follows the order of T3>T2>T1, but the gap between the ohmic resistances has widened. The mechanical instability in thick electrodes leads to an increase in contact resistance.

The active electrode is composed of a porous structure comprising active particles, conductive CB, and a binder, as illustrated in Figure S1. The electrolyte can directly penetrate the interior of the electrode through the porous structure, where electrochemical reactions occur on the surface of active particles, leading to the insertion of Li-ions into the active particles. Due to the relatively low conductivity of the active particles, it is necessary to enhance the conductivity of the active electrode by forming a conductive pathway composed of conductive CB and binder. This ensures the adhesion of the active electrode to the current collector, facilitating the conduction of electrons on the current collector. However, given the presence of numerous voids in the active electrode, complete contact between the active electrode and the current collector is not achieved, as depicted in Figure S1. The partially contacted state between the active electrode and the current collector may result in an increase in contact resistance due to the stress exerted by electrode expansion, leading to cracking and delamination in the contracted regions. This phenomenon hinders continuous electron conduction, resulting in elevated contact resistance.

Due to the volumetric expansion rate reaching up to 300% of Si material, significant von Mises stress occurs in both Si particles and the CBD regions during lithiation, as illustrated in Figure 7 A. This stress induces plastic deformation or damage to the CBD, preventing it from fully recovering to its original state as the particles contract during delithiation, thereby causing separation between Si particles and CBD. Consequently, the conductive agent fails to effectively transfer electrons to the Si particles, isolating them from the surrounding materials and resulting in the formation of free active particles. These free Si particles cannot adequately store Li, leading to a loss in electrode capacity. For thick electrodes, greater thickness increases the likelihood of abnormal compression between active particles (as shown in Figure 7B), exacerbated by higher charge-discharge rates, which in turn amplify capacity losses in thick electrodes under high rates.

Figure 8 A shows the surface of the un-lithiated Si electrode, which appears smooth and compact. After 50 cycles at 0.5 C, as depicted in Figure 8B, the 10-μm-thick electrode exhibits no cracks and no delamination at the edges, indicating overall good mechanical stability. Figure 8C illustrates the 20-μm-thick electrode where small cracks are visible both at the center and edges, with noticeable delamination at the edges. Figure 8D displays the 40-μm-thick electrode, which exhibits significant delamination where the active electrode layer separates from the Cu foil, accompanied by large cracks and areas of detachment on the active electrode surface. Figure 8E shows the distribution of by-products of electrolyte decomposition on Li-metal electrodes removed from dismantled coin cells after 5 min. The figure reveals a higher number of by-products of electrolyte decomposition on the Li-metal foil from the dismantled 40-μm-thick active electrode compared to that from the 10-μm-thick active electrode. This observation suggests that thicker electrodes may generate more heat due to higher current densities, leading to localized electrolyte failure, which is also one of the critical factors contributing to capacity loss. Both experimental observations and simulation predictions are consistent, indicating that thicker electrodes exhibit poorer mechanical stability and are more prone to cracking, fracturing, and delamination at high C-rates. Additionally, thicker electrodes experience greater electrolyte degradation.

Experimental results from the charge-discharge cycling of Si-based coin half-cells reveal significant differences in capacity retention for active electrodes of 10-, 20-, and 40-μm thickness at C-rates of 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5, as shown in Figures 9A–9F. As the C-rate increases from 0.1 to 0.5 C, the variation in capacity retention among electrodes of different thicknesses becomes more pronounced. For a given active electrode thickness, the capacity retention at 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C show minimal differences. Based on these experimental results, it is evident that capacity retention is less sensitive to changes in C-rate but more sensitive to changes in electrode thickness. This is consistent with the conclusions of PPM, which considers multi-field coupling, and indicates that thick active electrodes suffer from longer ion migration paths and mechanically unstable internal structures. High C-rates amplify these effects, leading to poorer capacity retention in thicker electrodes at higher C-rates. Conversely, for electrodes of the same thickness, the mechanical stability of the active electrode is less affected by varying C-rates (0.1, 0.3, and 0.5), resulting in minimal differences in capacity retention for the Si-based coin half-cells.

Thick electrodes can store more energy and exhibit higher overall energy density, but their increased thickness adversely affects the charge-discharge cycling life of the battery. This study establishes a 2D PPM that considers the multi-field coupling of mechanical, chemical, and electrical interactions and combines experimental analysis to evaluate the cycling performance of thick Si electrodes under different C-rates. Li intercalation-induced expansion in Si leads to compressive stress between Si particles, resulting in localized stress concentrations that affect the diffusion rate and the electrochemical reaction rate on the electrode surface. This causes an uneven concentration distribution on the Si particles, further impacting the stress distribution on the particles. On a macroscopic level, thick electrodes at high C-rates exhibit more significant concentration polarization, with Li-ions aggregating near the separator side of the electrolyte and depleting near the current collector side. This also results in significant variations in the solid-phase Li intercalation along the electrode thickness direction, leading to overall non-uniformity in Li intercalation. EIS experiments on coin half-cells with different thicknesses reveal that thicker electrodes exhibit higher contact impedance but lower charge transfer impedance. This indicates that the deterioration in electron-ion transport kinetics for thicker electrodes is due primarily to diffusion hindrance within the electrolyte. Moreover, thick electrodes generate larger tangential and normal stresses at the interface between the current collector and the active electrode, leading to a higher propensity for interface cracking and delamination. The von Mises stress generated between particles increases the stress within the CBD, raising the likelihood of CBD fracture. The thicker the electrode, the more likely damage is to occur. In summary, the capacity retention varies minimally under different C-rates for electrodes of the same thickness. However, as electrode thickness increases, the electrochemical performance and mechanical stability deteriorate at higher C-rates, resulting in a lower capacity retention rate.

CR2032-type coin Si-based half-cells were studied in this paper, and the internal structure is shown schematically in Figure 2A. The slurry was formulated through the mixing of deionized water, Si nanoparticles (BTR New Energy Materials) with an average diameter of 100 nm, sodium alginate (SA) binders (Sinopharm), and conductive CB (Super P, Timcal) in a mass ratio of 5:3:2. The slurry was subsequently applied onto Cu foil using a coating apparatus and then dried in a vacuum oven at 110°C for 40 min, resulting in solid layers with different thicknesses of around 10, 20, and 40 μm. These coated electrodes were further shaped into circular disks with a diameter of 10 mm utilizing a stamping device. Within a glove box environment characterized by an Ar atmosphere with water vapor and oxygen levels below 0.1 ppm, CR2032-type coin cells were assembled employing Li disks as electrodes. The half-cells utilized a 1.0-M LiPF6 commercial electrolyte (Tianci Materials Technology) in a solvent mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC):diethyl carbonate (DEC) at a ratio of 3:7 vol %, supplemented with 10 vol % fluoroethylene carbonate, and included a circular separator (2320, Celgard).

Ten electrode sheets were selected, and their combined weight and thickness were measured to determine the average weight and thickness per single electrode. The individual component weights were calculated based on the mass ratio, and subsequently, the electrode porosity was determined using the electrode void calculation formula as follows 18
,
33 :
(Equation 1)
ε el
= 1 −
ρ active
(
ω AM
ρ AM
+
ω B
ρ B
+
ω CA
ρ CA
)
,
where
ρ active
is the coating density of the active electrode,
ω AM
is the mass fraction of the active material,
ω B
is the mass fraction of the binder component,
ω CA
is the mass fraction of the conductive agent component,
ρ AM
is the density of the active material component,
ρ B
is the density of the binder component, and
ρ CA
is the density of the conductive agent component. The calculated porosity of the active electrode is determined to be 68%.

The 200-times charge-discharge cycling of the CR2032-type coin cells was performed on a battery testing system (CT-3008, Neware) at room temperature. Five charge-discharge cycles at a C-rate of 0.1 C in a voltage window of 0.01–1 V were first performed to form a “stable” SEI before the electrochemical cycling of the CR2032 coin cells. A total of 200 charge-discharge cycles were completed at constant currents of 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C. For Si-based half-cells, performance tends to degrade under high-rate conditions due to the significant expansion of the Si material. However, at lower rates, the performance difference is less pronounced. Therefore, 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 C rates were selected to investigate the rate performance. To ensure that cells with different electrode thicknesses are evaluated under consistent, capacity-based conditions, allowing for a fair comparison of their rate performance, the current represented by the C-rate was selected as the input current. 6
,
34
,
35 There may be some limitations in selecting C-rate as the research variable, but it is important to note that the electrode thickness varies in this study, which means the battery capacity also changes. The current at the same C-rate for different thicknesses will be different, and simply using the same current for comparison may overlook the issue of varying battery capacity, as capacity itself is a variable. By using the C-rate, the current and capacity have a fixed proportional relationship, making it more convenient for comparison from a normalized perspective. The discharge and charge cutoff voltages of the charge cycles were set to 0.01 and 1 V, respectively.

EIS testing of the coin cells was performed using an electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument), with the frequency scan range set from 0.1 to 100,000 Hz. EIS was conducted on the cells after the completion of the charge-discharge cycles while the cells were in a charged state.

Nanoindentation testing was performed using the Nano Indenter G200 (KLA Tencor, Oak Ridge, TN) equipped with a diamond Berkovich indenter (Young's modulus E = 1,141 GPa; Poisson's ratio ν = 0.07). In the quasi-static measurement mode, a controlled load of 5 mN was applied, creating 15 indentations per sample. The elastic modulus for each indentation was calculated using the standard Oliver-Pharr method. 36

After 50 cycles at the 0.5-C rate, the coin cell battery was disassembled using a coin cell disassembly device to extract the active electrode. The electrode was then dried with electrolyte and subsequently observed and photographed under a Zeiss Axioscope 7 Optical Microscope at 50× magnification.

The particle size statistics of 100-nm Si particles used in the experiment revealed a log-normal distribution of particle sizes for active Si particles; the histogram of particle size distribution is shown in Figure S2. This study utilized a log-normal distribution of particle sizes to generate circle particles and randomly distributed particles with sizes ranging from 80 to 120 nm on the active electrode, establishing a 2D PPM incorporating particle shape and distribution. The porosity of the Si electrode used in the experiment is 68%, with the Si particles accounting for approximately 16%. As a result, in the actual structure, the majority of the Si particles are encapsulated by the CBD. Additionally, to ensure convergence and computational efficiency in the PPM, direct contact between particles within the electrode is neglected. A minimum gap, equal to 10% of the sum of the radii of two adjacent particles, is introduced to prevent direct contact. Although the particles do not physically touch, load transfer between particles is still facilitated by the CBD surrounding them. Additionally, considering the 300% expansion rate of Si, setting a 10% particle radius distance has minimal impact on the calculation results. The particle-to-particle load is transferred through the CBD between them. Furthermore, it is better to analyze the Li-ion distribution in the electrode particles at different positions, such as near the current collector side, the middle of the electrode, and near the separator side for electrodes of different thicknesses.. The distribution process of particles was divided into two steps within the established electrode models of different thicknesses PPM: first, generating electrode particles with the same distributions near the current collector, near the separator, and in the middle of the electrode in various electrode thickness; second, supplementing new randomly distributed particle positions in the remaining locations. A schematic representation of the processes involved in the distribution of Si particles is shown in Figure S3A. The modeling process involves using MATLAB to establish a particle distribution model based on the actual particle size and distribution. Then, a multiphysics analysis model is constructed in COMSOL for further analysis.

According to the schematic diagram of the CR2032-type coin cell, the active electrode and the current collector are constrained axially between the anode shell and the Li foil, but there are no radial constraints. Therefore, during the lithiation expansion process, the active electrode and current collector predominantly expand in the radial direction, with minimal expansion in the thickness direction. Correspondingly, the delithiation contraction deformation also mainly occurs in the radial direction. Based on the structural analysis, an axisymmetric homogenized thin-plate model comprising Si, CBD, and voids is constructed to represent the CR2032-type coin cell, as shown in Figure S3B. In this model, the current collector and separator sides are set as roller supports, allowing the active electrode, current collector, and separator layers to move freely in the radial direction (r) but restricting their movement in the axial direction (z). Due to the softness of the separator material, the active electrode can also undergo slight displacement in the axial direction. The modulus of the porous matrix (CB, SA, and voids) was calculated using the methods outlined in Note S2. The parameters in the homogenization model such as modulus and partial molar volume of this model were obtained by PPM model simulation; the calculation process can be found in Note S3. The calculation of porosity changes can be referenced in Note S2.

The active particles and CBD were assumed linearly elastic in the PPM, and the ambient temperature was set to 298.15 K. The fitted equations of diffusion coefficients and transport coefficients obtained from experiments 37 with LiPF6 (3:7 EC:DEC) at 298.15 K are shown in Equations 2 and 3, and the relationship between ion conductivity (σ
e ) and electrolyte salts is shown in Figure S4. The other parameters for the PPM are given in Table S1.
(Equation 2)
D e
= 8.794 ×
10
− 17
c l 2
− 3.972 ×
10
− 13
c l
+ 4.862 ×
10
− 10
(Equation 3)
t +
= − 0.1287 ×
10
− 9
c l 3
+ 0.4106 ×
10
− 6
c l 2
− 0.4717 ×
10
− 3
c l
+ 0.4492

To gain a clearer understanding of the degree of Li insertion in particles on electrodes with different thicknesses, this paper represents the DOD of particles by using the integral of particle domain concentration on a 2D PPM and the ratio of the concentration domain to the maximum lithiation concentration, which is expressed as follows:
(Equation 4)
DOD =
∬ c d x d y
c s
A all
.
Here, A all represents the area of all active particles in PPM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Governing equations for the multiscale multiphysics model
ce:section.ce:para: Fully coupled relationship equations are provided in Notes S1–S3.
Requests for further information and resources should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Kai Zhang (kaizhang@tongji.edu.cn).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.

The code can be found online at https://github.com/GSL2013/Battery.git. Any additional information needed to reanalyze the data in the paper will be addressed by the lead contact upon reasonable request.
K.Z. is thankful for the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grant no. 12372173 and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai under grant no. 23ZR1468600.

S.G. designed the experiments, provided mathematics instruction for simulations, analyzed the data, and wrote the manuscript. J.Z. and B.T. designed and carried out the experiments. K.Z. and B.Z. conceptualized and supervised the project and provided the equipment and materials for the experiments. All authors have approved the final manuscript.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xcrp.2024.102305.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Document S1. Figures S1–S5, Table S1, and Notes S1–S3
Document S2. Article plus supplemental information
